question: What orders did McClellan now receive?
answer: To transfer his army to Aequia Creek, and put it under the command of Gen. Pope.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe Lee's campaign against Pope.
answer: After some manœuvring, Pope was compelled to fight the entire Confederate army on the old battle-field of Bull Run. The shattered remains of the Army of the Potomac retreated and took refuge within the fortifications at Washington.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the effect of this campaign?
answer: The Union army lost 30,000 men and a vast quantity of supplies. The capital was in great danger, with a victorious enemy outside and only broken battalions within.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What did Lee do?
answer: He crossed the Potomac and entered Maryland.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who assumed command of the Army of the Potomac after Pope's defeat?
answer: Gen. McClellan.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What notable battle was fought soon after?
answer: The battle of Antietam, September 17.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: After these battles, what course did Lee pursue?
answer: He retreated across the Potomac.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the effect of Lee's defeat?
answer: The North was saved from invasion and Washington from attack.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Why was McClellan again superseded, and by whom?
answer: For the slowness of his movements in pursuing the retreating army. Gen. Burnside took command.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the battle of Fredericksburg fought, and with what result?
answer: On December 13. The Union army was defeated with a loss of 12,000 men.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the Confederate victories during this year?
answer: Jackson's in the Shenandoah Valley; Lee's in the Peninsular campaign; the successful campaign against Pope; Bragg's raid in Kentucky; the battles of Cedar Mountain, Chickasaw Bluff and Fredericksburg.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the Union victories?
answer: The capture of Forts Henry, Donelson, Pulaski, Macon, Jackson, St. Phillip, Island No. 10, New Orleans, Roanoke Island, Newbern, Yorktown, Norfolk, Memphis, and the opening of the Mississippi to Vicksburg. Also the battles of Pea Ridge, Williamsburg, Fair Oaks, South Mountain, Antietam, Iuka, Corinth and Murfreesboro and the silencing of the Merrimac.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What terrible Indian massacre occurred?
answer: An outbreak in Minnesota, Iowa and Dakota. More than seven hundred whites were slain, and many thousands driven from their homes. Col. Sibley routed the Indians and took five hundred prisoners. Thirty-nine were hanged on one scaffold at Mankato, Minn., December 26.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the war plan of 1863, and how large was the force engaged?
answer: The plan was about the same as that of the preceding year. The Union force was some 700,000, and the Confederate 350,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the Emancipation Proclamation issued?
answer: January 1, 1863.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the second expedition against Vicksburg.
answer: After several weeks of fruitless effort on the north side of Vicksburg, Grant marched down the west bank of the river and crossed below the city. Between May 1 and 18 he defeated the Confederates at Fort Gibson, Jackson, Champion Hills, and Big Black River, and within seventeen days after he had landed Pemberton's army was shut up within the intrenchments of Vicksburg. After three desperate assaults the Union troops threw up intrenchments and began undermining the city. The siege lasted forty-seven days.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When did Vicksburg surrender?
answer: On the 4th of July.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What effect had this campaign?
answer: The Confederates lost the cities of Vicksburg and Jackson, with 37,000 prisoners and immense stores, and 10,000 of their troops killed and wounded. On the fall of Vicksburg, Port Hudson surrendered to Banks. The Mississippi was thus opened to the Gulf.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: After the battle of Murfreesboro, what were the movements in Tennessee?
answer: Little occurred until June, when Rosecrans, with 60,000 men, marched against Bragg and compelled him to evacuate Chattanooga. On September 8, 19 and 20 the battle of Chickamauga was fought. The Union army was defeated and withdrew to Chattanooga, while Bragg occupied the heights commanding the city, and threatened it with starvation.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was now the feeling in the North in regard to the Army of the Cumberland?
answer: One of great anxiety. The defeat of the Army of the Cumberland made it seem but a matter of time when the Confederacy would be finally victorious.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: By what means was the Army of the Tennessee relieved?
answer: Grant was appointed to succeed Rosecrans, and relief was obtained by Hooker's arrival with two corps from the Army of the Potomac, while Sherman hastened by forced marches from Iuka 200 miles away.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Where was Hooker, and how did he so suddenly appear before the Confederate army at Chattanooga?
answer: He was in Virginia. His army, 23,000 strong, came by rail in seven days, a distance of 1,200 miles.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the battles of Lookout Mountain and Missionary Ridge.
answer: The first movement was made by Gen. Thomas, who captured Orchard Knob on November 23. The following day Hooker charged the fortifications of Lookout Mountain. The Union forces carried the heights and swept over the crest, driving the enemy before them. The next morning Hooker advanced on the south of Missionary Ridge. Sherman was engaged on the northern flank. Grant, from his position on Orchard Knob, saw the effect of Sherman's assaults. The Confederate line in front of him was weakening, and with a bold movement Thomas's corps was launched against its centre. The orders were to take the rifle-pits at the foot of Missionary Ridge, then halt and reform; but in the excitement of success officers and men pushed forward and up the ascent. Grant caught the inspiration, and the entire force was ordered to engage in the conflict in one grand charge. Up they went, over rocks and chasms, all lines broken in the eagerness to reach the summit. Without firing a shot, and heedless of the tempest of missiles descending upon them, they gained the crest, captured the guns and turned them upon the retreating foe. That night the glow of the Federal camp-fires along the heights about Chattanooga crowned the most brilliant of Grant's achievements and the most picturesque of the battles of the war. This fight has become famous as the "battle above the clouds."
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What effect had these successes?
answer: The Union forces possessed Chattanooga, Bragg's army was routed and the General resigned. The victories gave control of East Tennessee, and furnished an open doorway by which the Union army gained easy access to Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Alabama.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: After the battle of Fredericksburg, who took command of the Army of the Potomac?
answer: Gen. Joseph Hooker, on January 26.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the battle of Chancellorsville fought?
answer: On May 2 and 3. The Union army was defeated with great loss.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: In this battle, what noted Confederate officer was killed?
answer: "Stonewall" Jackson. While returning from a reconnoissance at the front, he was fired upon by his own men, who mistook his escort for Federal cavalry.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who succeeded Hooker after the battle of Chancellorsville?
answer: Gen. George Meade.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the movements of Lee?
answer: With the flower of the Confederate army he crossed the Potomac, passed through Maryland, entered Pennsylvania, and proceeded within four miles of Harrisburg.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was his progress arrested?
answer: By the battle of Gettysburg, which was fought the 1st 2d and 3d of July, Gen. Meade commanding the Union forces. Gen. Lee retreated to Virginia.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: At the battle of Gettysburg, what were the casualties ?
answer: The Union loss was 23,000; the Confederate, 36,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: The loss of Vicksburg and the battle of Gettysburg had what effect on the Confederacy ?
answer: These losses and defeats turned the point of war, and the fortunes of the Confederacy began to wane.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How did Admiral Dupont attempt to take Charleston ?
answer: Having confidence in the ability of the iron-clads to resist cannon-balls, he attempted to run the fortifications and force his way up to the city. The attempt was a disastrous failure.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the Confederate successes for the year 1863 ?
answer: The Confederates gained the great battles of Chickamauga and Chancellorsville. The Union cause in Texas was depressed. Galveston was seized and every attack on Charleston successfully resisted.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the Union successes ?
answer: The Federals took Vicksburg and Port Hudson, and won the battles of Chattanooga and Gettysburg. Arkansas, East Tennessee, large parts of Louisiana and Mississippi and some parts of Texas were held by Union troops.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the Draft Riot ?
answer: Congress in 1863 passed a Conscription Act, and under this act the President ordered a draft for 300,000 men. This led to a three days' riot in New York City, beginning on July 13, and during which 150 lives were lost and a very large amount of property was destroyed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the raid made by General Judson Kilpatrick at the beginning of 1864.
answer: On the 28th of February Kilpatrick made a bold and successful raid upon the exterior defenses of Richmond. Railroad tracks were torn up, telegraph wires cut, six large grist mills were destroyed and heavy damage was done. At a point three miles from Richmond he found the enemy's works too strong to be assailed by a force of cavalry and withdrew, camping that night within six miles of the Confederate capital.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was accomplished by this expedition?
answer: Many miles of railroad were cut, several hundred prisoners were captured, and $7,000,000 worth of property was destroyed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What befell Colonel Dahlgren and his command?
answer: While attempting to return, after destroying the grist mills on the James River Canal, Dahlgren fell into an ambush of the enemy and was killed. His remains were treated with indignity.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was General Grant appointed Lieutenant-General of the Union Army?
answer: March 3, 1864.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Where was the main strength of the Confederacy?
answer: It lay in the armies of Robert E. Lee in Virginia and Joseph E. Johnston in Georgia.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What plan was now adopted?
answer: Grant was to march against Lee, and Sherman to attack Johnston and sweep through to the coast. 543. Describe Sherman's advance upon Atlanta. He started with a force of 100,000 men. For one hun-dred miles there was continuous skirmishing. Sherman would drive Johnston into a stronghold, and then with consummate skill would outflank him, when Johnston with equal skill would retreat to a new post and prepare to meet his opponent again. Several bloody battles were fought, and finally Johnston retired on July 10 to the intrenchments of Atlanta. Hood was now put in command.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When did Atlanta surrender?
answer: September 2, 1864.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What had been the loss during this campaign of four months' fighting?
answer: There had been ten pitched battles and scores of minor engagements, at a cost to the Union army of 30,000 and to the Confederacy of about 40,000 men.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What movement was made by Hood, and why?
answer: He turned to invade Tennessee, with the expectation that Sherman would follow him, and that thus Georgia would be saved from invasion.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What became of Hood's army?
answer: Hood marched against Gens. Thomas and Schofield at Nashville. After severe fighting, Thomas withdrew from the fortifications and remained two weeks. He then suddenly burst forth and drove the Confederate army out of its intrenchments and into headlong flight. It was completely demoralized, and, for further use, destroyed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe Sherman's "March to the Sea," after the burning of Atlanta.
answer: Hood being moved from his path, there was little to impede his progress. With 60,000 troops, in five weeks he marched three hundred miles, and then captured Savannah.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What effect had this march on Georgia?
answer: A fertile region, sixty miles wide and three hundred miles long, was devastated, and three hundred miles of railroad were destroyed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: At the time of Grant's move towards Richmond, what battles were fought?
answer: The battles of the Wilderness and Spottsylvania.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the battle of the Wilderness.
answer: After crossing the Rapidan, the Union army plunged into the Wilderness, where it was attacked on May 5, by the Confederate army. In the woods, dense with smoke, this strangest of battles was fought. The third day both armies, worn out by the desperate struggle, remained in their intrenchments. Grant lost 20,000 men, Lee 10,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What did Grant do, and what battle followed?
answer: He pushed his army by the Confederate right flank towards Spottsylvania Court House, where for five days, from May 8 to 12, terrible fighting occurred. Ten thousand men fell on each side.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Where was the next engagement?
answer: Grant now concluded to try the flank movement again, and pushed forward to Cold Harbor, a short distance from Richmond. Lee hastened by a shorter route, and arrived in time to prepare for defense. Early on the morning of June 4 the Union army made an assault on Cold Harbor. Twenty minutes after the first shot was fired, fully ten thousand Union men were killed or wounded, while the enemy's loss was little over one thousand.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the attack on Petersburg?
answer: The Confederate works could not be carried, so Grant threw up intrenchments, and prepared for a siege of Richmond.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the loss in this campaign?
answer: The Union army lost 70,000. and the Confederates 40,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What memorable words were written by General Grant to the President on the 11th of May, 1864 ?
answer: "I propose to fight it out on this line, if it takes all summer."
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: During the siege of Richmond, what two important events occurred?
answer: The Mine Explosion at Petersburg, Va., and the capture of the Weldon Railroad.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the mine explosion?
answer: A mine was dug beneath a strong Confederate fort, and was fired with a blast of 8,000 pounds of powder. The fort and garrison were destroyed. At the same time the Union artillery opened along the line, and an assaulting column rushed forward; but it stopped in the crater produced by the explosion. The Confederates, rallying, turned their artillery upon the mass of men within the demolished fort, and about 4,000 were lost before the rest could retreat to the Union lines. This is known as the "Battle of the Crater."
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What raids were made by General Early?
answer: Early entered Maryland, threatened Washington and Baltimore, defeated Wallace, and then withdrew to Virginia. In the same month — July — he crossed into Pennsylvania, his troops setting fire to Chambersburg; but after that he immediately withdrew.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was secured by these expeditions?
answer: A vast amount of stores, five thousand horses, and a withdrawal of part of Grant's army from before Petersburg.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What did General Sheridan do?
answer: In September, Sheridan defeated Early at Winchester and Fisher's Hill, and in a week destroyed half the enemy and put the rest to flight. Early returned with reinforcements, and, under cover of a dense fog, surprised Sheri- Early's army at Cedar Creek, October 19, and drove it back in confusion. At this critical moment Sheridan arrived from Winchester, "twenty miles away," checked the retreating columns, turned them "about face" and won the battle.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the effect of Sheridan's successes?
answer: This was the most brilliant campaign of the war. In one month Sheridan had virtually annihilated Early's army. His own loss was 17,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the Red River expedition.
answer: Gen. Banks was sent up the Red River to loosen the Confederate grasp on that region and Texas. This campaign resulted in a Confederate triumph. Banks lost 5,000 men, 18 guns, and large supplies.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the massacre of Fort Pillow, Ky.?
answer: On April 12. The Confederates were maddened by the sight of negro troops opposing them, and a terrible massacre followed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What brilliant naval victory did Admiral Farragut achieve?
answer: The capture of Mobile Harbor, August 5. He closed it against blockade-runners. The city of Mobile was not captured until April 12, 1865.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the expedition against Fort Fisher.
answer: Fort Fisher defended the Harbor of Wilmington, N.C. The expedition consisted of seventy vessels under Commodore Porter, and a land force under Gen. Butler. After a fierce bombardment, Dec. 24 and 25, Butler decided that the fort could not be taken by assault, and the army returned to Fortress Monroe. Commodore Porter asked for another trial. The same troops were sent back under Gen. Terry. By a series of trenches a column of troops worked themselves up to within two hundred yards of the fortifications. At the word they rushed forward and burst into the fort. The hand-to-hand conflict within lasted for hours. Late at night the garrison surrendered, on January 15, 1865.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What occurred at St. Albans, Vermont, on the 19th of October, 1864?
answer: A band of raiders from Canada assaulted the town, robbed the banks, seized horses from the livery stables and returned to Canada before the inhabitants recovered from their surprise. The entire raid was accomplished in a half-hour.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the attempt of Confederate emissaries to burn New York.
answer: On the night of November 25, 1864, an attempt was made to burn New York by saturating beds in the principal hotels with a mixture of phosphorus and oil. The attempt was discovered in season and a great disaster averted.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the Alabama, and what damage did she do?
answer: She was a British steamer built in England, but officered and commissioned by the Confederate government. Her object was to prey upon Union commerce, and she succeeded in capturing more than sixty vessels. Her commander was Capt. Raphael Semmes.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When, where, and with what result was the encounter between the Kearsarge and the Alabama?
answer: The engagement between the Kearsarge and the Alabama took place off the coast of France, in the English Channel, June 15, 1864. The Alabama was sunk. Capt. John A. Winslow, commanding the Kearsarge, rescued a part of the sinking crew. The English yacht Deerhound picked up the remainder and steamed off to the British coast. Capt. Semmes was among the number thus rescued.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What other interesting naval operation occurred during this year?
answer: The destruction of the Confederate iron-clad Albemarle, at Plymouth, North Carolina. This was accomplished by Lieut. Cushing, who fastened a torpedo to the Albemarle, which exploded and sunk her.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: At the close of 1864, what was the political condition of the North?
answer: There was much dissatisfaction at the North with the conduct of the war; the debt was becoming enormous and paper money greatly depreciated. Grant had been repulsed at Cold Harbor and Early had made successful raids. In the midst of these calamities Abraham Lincoln was renominated by the Republicans, and the Democrats nominated Geo. B. McClellan, who stood firmly for the prosecution of the war but was not in full sympathy with the policy of the administration. Lincoln's popular majority was more than four hundred thousand.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How did gold compare in value with greenbacks?
answer: In July, 1864, it required $2.90 in paper to buy one dollar in gold.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the Confederate victories during this year?
answer: The Confederates won the battles of Sabine Cross Roads, Wilderness, Bermuda Hundreds, Spottsylvania, New Market and Cold Harbor. They resisted the Red River and Florida expeditions, two attacks upon Petersburg and one against Fort Fisher.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Name the Union victories.
answer: They won the battle of Atlanta and the battles preceding it — Dalton, Resaca, Dallas, Lost Mountain, Kenesaw Mountain, Winchester, Fisher's Hill, Cedar Creek and Nashville. They captured Fort de Russy, the forts in Mobile Harbor, and Fort McAllister; Sheridan annihilated Early's army; Sherman marched across Georgia and took Savannah; Thomas destroyed Hood's army, and the Confederacy was almost extinguished; only North and South Carolina remained in full control of the Confederacy.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the plan of the campaign for 1865?
answer: Sherman was to move north from Savannah against Johnston, and then join Grant in a final attack upon Lee. Sheridan had swept down from the Shenandoah, cut the railroads north of Richmond, and formed in line before Petersburg. Wilson was in Alabama and Georgia, while Stoneman held the passes of the Alleghanies and waited in North Carolina for the issue in Virginia.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were General Sherman's movements?
answer: Early in February Sherman started north, and, though meeting with untold difficulties, swept on. He captured Columbia on Feb. 17, and on the following day Charleston was evacuated. Johnston fiercely opposed him at Averysboro and Bentonville, but was defeated. On the banks of the Neuse Sherman met Schofield and Terry, and the three, with a combined army of 100,000 men, waited for the final charge against the Confederacy. The distance traveled was more than 425 miles, one hundred of which was swamp.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the siege of Richmond.
answer: Lee was now shut up in Richmond, and his only hope of prolonging the struggle was to cut his way out. He decided to attack Grant's lines, cause a concentration of troops, and retreat by other routes before his plan was discovered. He failed, as Grant returned the advance with renewed vigor, capturing the outer defenses and preventing a retreat. 579. Give a description of the battle of Five Forks. Sheridan, with his cavalry and heavy columns of infantry, pushed out from Grant's left wing and threw a heavy force behind the Confederate position at Five Forks. Assailed in every direction, the garrison was overwhelmed, and five thousand men were taken prisoners.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What effect had this brilliant victory?
answer: Lee's position was made untenable. His right was turned and his rear threatened.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the capture of Petersburg and Richmond.
answer: On the morning of April 3, the Union army advanced in an overwhelming assault along the whole front. By noon the Confederate line of intrenchments, before which the Army of the Potomac had lain so long, was broken, and thousands of prisoners were captured. That night Petersburg and Richmond were evacuated. The next morning the Union troops took possession of the Confederate capital.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When and to whom did Lee and Johnston surrender?
answer: Lee surrendered to General Grant at Appomattox, near Lynchburg, Va., on April 9, and Johnston to Sherman in North Carolina on April 26.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When and how was Jefferson Davis captured, and what was done with him?
answer: He was arrested by a party of Union troops, in Georgia, on May 10, 1865, and imprisoned in Fortress Monroe, but was later released on bail furnished by Horace Greeley.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How long did the Civil War last?
answer: A little more than four years.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When and by whom was Abraham Lincoln assassinated?
answer: On the 14th of April, 1865, by John Wilkes Booth, at Ford's Theatre in Washington, D.C.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What became of Booth?
answer: After shooting the President he sprang upon the stage. His spur caught in a flag used to drape the proscenium, and he was thrown heavily, breaking his leg. He escaped, mounted his horse and fled through Maryland, into Virginia, where he was overtaken, near Bowling Green, in a barn and shot.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Give an account of the attack on Secretary Seward.
answer: On the same night of the assassination of Lincoln an accomplice stabbed Mr. Seward while lying ill in bed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What became of Booth's accomplices?
answer: Harold, Payne, Atzerodt, and Mrs. Surratt were hanged; Arnold, Mudd and O'Laughlin were imprisoned for life, and Spangler was sentenced for six years.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the total number of troops called out in the war, and what the total number obtained?
answer: The number called out was 2,942,748, the number obtained was 2,690,401.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How many men were lost in the war?
answer: The Union armies probably lost in battle, or through its effects, 300,000 men, while 200,000 were crippled for life. The Confederate loss is not positively known, but was undoubtedly almost as heavy.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the cost of the war?
answer: The government's debt on August 31, 1865, was nearly $2,844,000,000. The daily expenses at one time reached the sum of $2,500,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was slavery forever abolished in the United States?
answer: By the thirteenth amendment to the Constitution, which was ratified on December 18, 1865.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who succeeded Lincoln as President?
answer: Andrew Johnson. He was sworn in on the day of Lincoln's death, April 15, 1865.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What caused trouble between Johnson and Congress?
answer: The exercise of the veto power.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the most important bills passed over the President's veto?
answer: The Freedmen's Bureau, the Civil Rights, and the Tenure of Office bills.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the nature of these bills?
answer: The Freedmen's Bureau bill provided for the establishment of a department for the protection of the freedmen. The Civil Rights bill guaranteed to the negroes the full rights of citizenship. The Tenure of Office bill made it necessary that the consent of the Senate shall be obtained for the President's removal of any person from a civil office.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: On what charges was Johnson impeached?
answer: Various misdemeanors, and contempt of the Senate by violating the Tenure of Office act in an attempt to remove arbitrarily Edwin M. Stanton, Secretary of War. Johnson was acquitted, the two-thirds majority necessary for conviction lacking one vote.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What is the fourteenth amendment to the Constitution? When adopted?
answer: It guarantees equal civil rights to all, and bases representation in each of the States on the number of voters. It was adopted July 28, 1868.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What demand upon France was made by the United States government?
answer: The recall from Mexico of the French troops under Maximilian.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What States were added to the Union during the war?
answer: West Virginia and Nevada.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the Reconstruction policy of Johnson?
answer: He recognized the State governments that had been formed during the war under the protection of the Union army in Virginia, Tennessee, Arkansas and Louisiana. In the other States he appointed provisional governors and authorized the calling of conventions to form loyal governments. These conventions met, repealed the ordinances of secession, repudiated the Confederate debt, and ratified the thirteenth amendment. On these conditions Johnson claimed that the States, having never been legally out of the Union, should be restored to their rights in the Union. Pardon was proclaimed to those persons who had been engaged in secession with certain exceptions.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was Universal Amnesty declared?
answer: On Christmas day, 1868.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the Reconstruction policy of Congress?
answer: Congress decided that the President had no power to prescribe the conditions for the readmission of the seceded States, and his proclamations and orders were treated as invalid. Tennessee was admitted, but the other States were placed under military rule. After a bitter and protracted struggle, governments were finally established in Arkansas, Alabama, Florida, Louisiana, North Carolina and South Carolina.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the ''iron-clad oath,'' and who were the so-called ''carpet-baggers''?
answer: Congress made a requirement that every candidate for holding office should swear that he had not participated in the secession movement. As but few Southerners could conscientiously take this oath it was called "iron-clad," and the offices were filled by men who had come from the North during the war and soon after it. Owing to their recent arrival, these men were termed "carpet-baggers."
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How long was the government in establishing its Reconstruction policy?
answer: Three years. The Southern States were not represented in the government from 1861 to 1868. Virginia, Mississippi and Texas were not allowed to vote for President until after Grant's first administration.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What proclamation was issued by Johnson in regard to the Fenians?
answer: The Fenians were making preparations to cross the Canadian frontier at Buffalo, N.Y., and St. Albans, Vt. Johnson issued a proclamation declaring the movement a violation of neutrality, and sent General Meade to execute the law.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was Alaska obtained?
answer: By purchase from Russia, in October, 1867, for $7,200,000 in gold. It contains 500,000 square miles.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What treaty was made with China?
answer: In 1868 an embassy from China, in charge of Anson Burlingame, then our diplomatic representative in China, visited the United States. This was the first event of its kind in the history of the Chinese nation. A treaty was framed granting valuable commercial privileges.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What repeated demand was made of the French government?
answer: During our civil war France attempted to establish an empire in Mexico, and Maximilian, an Archduke of Austria, was appointed emperor. The United States government protested, but with its hands full of its own affairs was unable to enforce the Monroe Doctrine. After the close of our war our government again demanded of Napoleon the recall of French troops. Maximilian, deprived of foreign aid, was defeated by the Mexicans, and on June 19, 1867, was shot after a trial by his captors.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the Atlantic cable completed?
answer: In June, 1866. The first cable was attempted in 1856, and laid from New York to St. Johns, Newfoundland. In 1857 a vain effort was made to cross the ocean. In 1858 a cable was laid across the Atlantic and apparently worked successfully, but on September 1 it ceased to work. Cyrus W. Field, the projector of this enterprise, was not discouraged, and organized a third company, which in June, 1866, was successful. There are now several cables.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the political situation at that time?
answer: The Republican party nominated Ulysses S. Grant and Schuyler Colfax; the Democrats, Horatio Seymour and Frank P. Blair. Virginia, Mississippi and Texas had not yet been "reconstructed" and were not allowed to vote.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the Union Pacific Railway completed? How far is it from New York to San Francisco?
answer: In 1869. The distance from New York to San Francisco is about 3,300 miles.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was the fifteenth amendment formally proclaimed, and what is its nature?
answer: On March 30, 1870, and it provided that the right of the citizens of the United States to vote shall not be denied or abridged by the United States or any State, on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the important conflagrations of 1871 and 1872.
answer: A great fire broke out in Chicago on Sunday night, October 8, 1871. It was the most terrible conflagration in modern times. Three thousand acres of almost solid blocks were devastated. Twenty-five thousand buildings were burned, $200,000,000 worth of property was destroyed, and 100,000 persons were rendered homeless. Contributions to the amount of more than $7,500,000 were sent to the suffering citizens from nearly all parts of the world. During the same autumn fires raged in the forests of Wisconsin, Minnesota and Michigan. Entire villages were consumed. In Wisconsin alone nearly 1,500 persons perished. On November 9, 1872, fire swept over sixty acres in the whole-sale trade centre of Boston, and destroyed $70,000,000 worth of property.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the Alabama claims difficulty with England, and how was it settled?
answer: The English government refused to pay for the damages to American commerce caused by the cruising of the Alabama. The dispute was settled by arbitration, in a convention at Geneva, Switzerland, the English government paying $15,500,000 to the United States.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was the dispute regarding the Northwestern boundary between the United States and British America adjusted?
answer: It was submitted to the Emperor of Germany and was decided in favor of the United States.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the proposed annexation of San Domingo?
answer: The republic of San Domingo, comprising a large part of the island of Hayti, applied for admission to the United States, but was rejected. A commission appointed by the President to examine its condition reported favorably, but the measure was rejected by Congress.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: At the close of Grant's first term, what was the political situation?
answer: Grant was renominated by the Republicans, and the Democrats endorsed the nomination of Horace Greeley, already the Liberal-Republican candidate.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Give a short biography of Horace Greeley.
answer: Horace Greeley was one of our great self-made men. Born in poverty, he had risen from the humblest position to be the most renowned editor of the age. In 1841 he had founded the New York Tribune. When civil war seemed imminent, he had urged measures to avoid it, even advocating the policy of letting the discontented States depart in peace; but when war had been proclaimed he had urged a vigorous prosecution. At the close of the war he had pleaded for immediate conciliation. Just before the close of the Presidential canvass his wife died, and this, together with the desertion of friends and the excitement of the contest, unsettled his mind. He was confined in a private asylum, where he soon died.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the difficulties with the Modoc Indians.
answer: Having refused to stay upon their reservation in Oregon, troops were sent against them, whereupon they retreated to their fastnesses in the Lava Beds. Peace commissioners, proposing to arrange the difficulty, held a conference with the chiefs. In the midst of this council, the Indians treacherously murdered General Canby and the Rev. Dr. Thomas and wounded Mr. Meacham. The Modocs were then bombarded and forced to surrender. Captain Jack and several of the leaders of the band were hanged at Fort Klamath, October 3, 1873.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the panic of 1873.
answer: In the autumn of 1873 Jay Cooke & Co., bankers, of Philadelphia, became too heavily involved in railroad enterprises, especially the Northern Pacific, and failed. The failure produced a financial crisis, and hundreds of prominent firms all over the Union were involved in ruin. The money market became stringent, and a stagnation of business followed. But for the timely aid of the government in throwing several millions of dollars into the market, the distress would have been much greater. The name "Black Friday" was applied to the day when almost every man and firm in business seemed to be threatened with bankruptcy.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What centennial anniversaries were observed with great enthusiasm in 1875 and 1876?
answer: The hundredth anniversary of the battle of Lexington, of the battle of Bunker Hill, and of the signing of the Declaration of Independence.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the main features of the Centennial Exhibition.
answer: Fairmount Park, in Philadelphia, was the scene of the display. More than two hundred buildings were scattered over the extensive ground, the main structure covering twenty acres. Exhibits were gathered from every clime. The exhibition lasted six months, from May to November, and the total number of visitors was 9,910,966.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What war with the Sioux Indians occurred, and what terrible defeat overtook the government forces?
answer: A band of Sioux took the war-path. Troops were sent to force them to return to their reservation. On the 25th of June, 1876, General Custer suddenly came upon the enemy and a desperate fight followed, in which Custer and his entire command were killed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: In 1876, who were nominated for President?
answer: The Republicans nominated Rutherford B. Hayes and Wm. A. Wheeler; the Democrats, Samuel J. Tilden and Thomas A. Hendricks; the Greenback party, Peter Cooper and Samuel F. Cary.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the election?
answer: Both Republicans and Democrats claimed the election. the Republicans insisting that the States of South Carolina, Florida and Louisiana had been carried by the Democrats by fraud.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was Rutherford B. Hayes chosen President?
answer: In order to reach a settlement, a Joint Electoral Commission was appointed, composed of five United States Senators, five United States Representatives, and five Justices of the Supreme Court. This body decided that 185 electoral votes had been cast for Hayes and Wheeler, and 184 for Tilden and Hendricks.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the feeling among the American people in regard to the settlement of this question?
answer: The Republicans were jubilant, while the Democrats denounced the decision, claiming that the Commission had decided, not by evidence, but on partisan grounds, as eight of them were Republicans and seven were Democrats; therefore Hayes was termed by his opponents the "eight-to-seven" President.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When Samuel J. Tilden was asked if his party should stand by his election, what reply did he make?
answer: To the delegation who sought his advice he insisted that no act of theirs should imperil the country.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What promise was made by Hayes in regard to "rotation in office"?
answer: To make "no dismissal except for cause, and no promotion except for merit."
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was President Hayes's Southern policy?
answer: One of conciliation. The Federal troops which had hitherto sustained the Republican State governments in South Carolina and Louisiana were withdrawn and Democratic officers at once took control of the local affairs He UNITED STATES HISTORY. also appointed Judge Key, a Southerner and an ex-Confederate, Postmaster General.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What railroad strike occurred in 1877?
answer: On nearly all of the principal railroads in the Northern States a strike of workmen occurred, the cause being a general reduction of wages. At Pittsburg, Pa., the strike grew into a terrible riot, in the course of which vast amounts of property were destroyed; it was quelled only by the use of troops. In the riot more than one hundred lives were sacrificed, and the Pennsylvania Railroad suffered a loss of nearly $3,000,000 worth of property.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the Bland Silver Bill?
answer: In 1873 Congress demonetized silver, and made gold the sole standard of our currency. Opposition arose to this act, and in 1878 a bill was passed remonetizing silver and making the standard silver dollar a legal tender for the payment of debts. The government was required to purchase from $2,000,000 to $4,000,000 worth of silver bullion every month, and coin it into dollars. The bill was called the "Bland bill," because Representative Bland of Missouri set it in motion in Congress.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the yellow fever of 1878.
answer: In the summer of 1878 it broke out in New Orleans and spread with alarming rapidity northward along the Mississippi into Missouri and Tennessee. More than 7,000 deaths were reported.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When did the resumption of specie payments occur?
answer: January 1, 1879. On December 17, 1878, gold, for the first time since January, 1862, sold in New York at par.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What Indian difficulties took place in 1879?
answer: The Ute Indians at the White River Agency, being dissatisfied on account of the encroachments of miners and the non-payment of money promised by the govern ment, took up arms and massacred and abused the whites at the agency. The revolt was suppressed by United States troops.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: In 1880, what was the population of the United States?
answer: The census of 1880 showed a population of more than 50,000,000. The increase of inhabitants from 1870 to 1880 was some 12,000,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the Fishery Awards?
answer: Difficulties sprang up between Great Britain and the United States because of the fisheries on our northeast coast. The matter was left to an international commission of arbitration which awarded Great Britain the sum of $5,500,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were our treaties with China in 1880?
answer: One in relation to commerce, and the other granting to our government the regulation of Chinese immigration.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Was President Hayes's administration favorable to his party?
answer: A large element in the party were not satisfied with his policy of conciliation toward the South. They believed that, if the electoral vote had legally been cast for him as President, it must also have been cast for the Republican State officers, and that it was the duty of the President to protect those officers by the presence of United States troops at the State capitals.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who were the nominees for the Presidency to succeed President Hayes?
answer: The Republicans nominated James A. Garfield and Chester A. Arthur. The Democrats nominated Winfield S. Hancock and William H. English. The Greenback-Labor party nominated James B. Weaver and Benjamin I. Chambers.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the principal issue between the Republican and Democratic parties?
answer: The tariff question.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the principles advocated by the Greenback Labor party?
answer: The Greenback-Labor party was opposed to the issue of paper money by any private corporation; they insisted that it should be issued solely by the government and made a full legal tender. They claimed also that monopolies were fast controlling all kinds of business and that labor was becoming a mere slave to the avarice and greed of capital; that legislation was enacted for the rich without due regard for the poor; and that our factories were over-burdensome to women and children.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was Garfield inaugurated President?
answer: March 4, 1881.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What gigantic swindling system was detected in the Post-office Department?
answer: The Star Route frauds, whereby contracts in the West for carrying the mails were so manipulated that great wrongs and robberies were perpetrated. Within two months contracts to the amount of nearly $2,000,000 were annulled. The alleged conspirators were tried, but judgment was never rendered against them.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was President Garfield assassinated?
answer: On the morning of July 2, 1881, by Charles J. Guiteau, at a railway station in Washington, D.C.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the feeling of the country in regard to the assassination, and what became of the assassin?
answer: The assassination produced profound and universal sorrow. Preparations for the celebration of Independence Day were generally abandoned. President Garfield died on the 19th of September following, at Long Branch, N.J. He is buried at Cleveland, O. The assassin was tried, convicted and hanged.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What testimonial of respect was shown by the Queen of England to Mrs. Garfield?
answer: By direction of the Queen a wreath of flowers was placed upon Garfield's coffin, as a sign of her sympathy with the martyred President's wife.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When did Chester A. Arthur become President of the United States?
answer: Immediately on being informed of the President's death he took the oath of office.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the extent of the flood in the Mississippi Valley in 1882?
answer: Nearly 100,000 persons were rendered homeless.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Describe the East River Bridge.
answer: A suspension bridge connecting New York City and Brooklyn, 5,989 feet long, was opened to the use of the public in 1883. It was begun January 3, 1870. The height of the towers is 278 feet and the length of the suspended span from tower to tower 1,596 feet. The height from the water, at the center, is 135 feet. The four great cables are 15¾ inches in diameter, and each cable contains 5,296 parallel wires of galvanized steel, oil-coated, closely wrapped, and weighing, with covering, 897½ tons.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What is the Civil Service Act, and when was it passed?
answer: It was passed in 1883, and aimed to regulate appointments and promotions in the civil service of the United States by means of examinations instead of political preferment.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was letter postage reduced from three cents to two cents?
answer: October 1, 1883.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was Alaska recognized as a Territory?
answer: In 1884 a bill was approved by which a governor, judge, marshal and other officers were to be appointed for a term of four years, but no legislative assembly or Congressional delegate was authorized. Sitka was made the temporary seat of government.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How was Chinese immigration regulated?
answer: It was forbidden by a law passed in 1882, for ten years.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who were nominated for President in 1884?
answer: The Democrats nominated Grover Cleveland and Thomas A. Hendricks. The Republicans, James G. Blaine and John A. Logan. The Greenback-Labor party, B. F. Butler and A. M. West. The Prohibitionists, John P. St. John and William Daniel.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the chief subjects of discussion in this campaign?
answer: Between the Democrats and Republicans, the tariff and scandalous personalities. The Greenback-Labor party espoused the same policies as four years before, while the Prohibitionists demanded the suppression of the liquor traffic by the general government.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who was elected President, and by what popular majority? Which State held the deciding vote?
answer: Grover Cleveland. New York State held the deciding vote, and its Democratic majority was about 1,045.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When did General Grant die?
answer: July 23, 1885, at Mount McGregor, N.Y.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Where and when did Vice-President Hendricks die?
answer: At his home in Indianapolis, November 25, 1885.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How many times has the Vice-Presidency been made vacant by death?
answer: Five times.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What other noted men died shortly after Mr. Hendricks?
answer: George B. McClellan, Winfield S. Hancock, Charles Francis Adams and Chester A. Arthur.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What notable events occurred in 1886?
answer: The Haymarket riot in Chicago, May 3. The riot of Milwaukee, May 6. The earthquake of Charleston, S.C. The conviction of eight Anarchists in Chicago. The rise of the Knights of Labor to prominence. Great gales off the coast of Texas, and wrecks on Lakes Michigan and Superior. The unveiling of the statue, "Liberty Enlightening the World," in New York Harbor.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What noted man died December 26, 1886?
answer: John A. Logan, United States Senator from Illinois.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What important commercial act was passed by Congress in January of this year?
answer: The Inter-State Commerce Act. It was signed by the President, February 4, 1887.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What bill originated in the Senate in regard to the Canadian fishery question?
answer: The Retaliation Act which placed in the hands of the President the power to prohibit the importation of the products of the Canadian fisheries, or, if necessary, to suspend all commerce with Canada until the differences between the United States and that country were settled.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the election of 1888?
answer: The Republicans elected Benjamin Harrison President, and Levi P. Morton Vice-President. The election was exciting and majorities in the doubtful States small.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: In February, 1889, what important office was added to the Cabinet?
answer: Secretary of Agriculture.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What legislation in the same month was of special interest to the people of the Northwest?
answer: An act by which North and South Dakota, Washington and Montana were admitted as States.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What interesting anniversary occurred in 1889?
answer: The centenary of the first inauguration of Washington as President of the United States. President Harrison in a proclamation had recommended that on "Thursday, April 30, at the hour of nine in the morning, the people of the entire country repair to their respective places of Divine worship, to implore the favor of God that the blessings of liberty, prosperity, and peace may abide with us as a people." This recommendation met with general observance. The day was also celebrated by patriotic displays in all the cities and principal towns of the Union.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What important tariff and silver legislation was enacted in 1890?
answer: "The McKinley law," so called, made important changes in the tariff, generally in the direction of an increase in the rate of protective duties. The Sherman act was a law repealing the Bland act, and requiring the government to purchase monthly 4,500,000 ounces of silver bullion at the market price and coin a part of it into standard dollars.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What law did Congress pass in 1891 bearing upon copyright ?
answer: A law providing that the President may, under certain conditions, by proclamation extend to foreigners the privileges of copyright in the United States.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How near to the verge of war did the United States come during Harrison's administration?
answer: Some American sailors were murderously attacked in the streets of Valparaiso by a Chilian mob. Our government demanded reparation in damages and an apology, under pain of war. Chili was just coming out of a civil war of her own, and for reasons of domestic policy held back a little, but succumbed just as the period fixed in the American ultimatum was expiring.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the effects of the McKinley law?
answer: Its opponents declared that the operation of the "McKinley law" resulted in a general advance in the cost of living. Its supporters claimed that it increased the general prosperity. At the next general election, the Republicans lost the House of Representatives and were supplanted by a large Democratic majority.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the election of 1892?
answer: The Democrats elected Grover Cleveland President and Adlai E. Stevenson Vice-President. The campaign was notable for the absence of much of the usual excitement. The general verdict appeared to be against a continuance of the high protective rates of the McKinley tariff.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What event of international interest marked the year 1893?
answer: The World's Columbian Exposition, held at Chicago commemorating the 400th anniversary of the discovery of America by Columbus.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Did any exceptional political occurrences mark this year?
answer: (a) President Cleveland convened Congress in extra session in August to consider the alarming financial depression which had recently set in. This session ended with the repeal of the silver-purchase clause of the Sherman Act of 1890. (b) A dispute between this country and England concerning the seal industry in the Bering Sea, which had been assuming a threatening aspect, was amicably settled by international arbitration.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What important question engaged the attention of the Congress and the people in 1894?
answer: A protracted discussion by Congress (Democratic) resulted in the enactment of a revised tariff law repealing the duties on imported wools, and other important raw materials, and reducing duties on many manufactured or partly manufactured articles.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the Congressional elections of 1894?
answer: A general "overturn," replacing the Democratic majority in the House of Representatives by a larger Republican majority.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What occurrences in the labor-field excited general anxiety in 1894?
answer: A strike of railroad employees at Chicago assumed an almost revolutionary character by reason of violence, incendiarism, and general lawlessness. President Cleveland ordered United States troops to the scene of the troubles, thus terminating the episode.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What great question was brought most prominently to the attention of the people in 1895-1896?
answer: The general question of the settlement by arbitration of all differences with other nations. A dispute between Great Britain and Venezuela, concerning the boundary line between Venezuela and British Guiana, drew forth from President Cleveland a sharp protest, coupled with an intimation that our government, in support of the "Monroe Doctrine," would, if necessary, go to war. After considerable diplomatic correspondence it was agreed to submit the Venezuelan boundary question to arbitration, which our government pronounced satisfactory.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the result of the general election of 1896?
answer: The Republicans elected William McKinley President and Garrett A. Hobart Vice-President, with a working majority in the House of Representatives, on platform declarations assuring the maintenance of the existing gold standard in our currency. The Democrats had demanded the unlimited free coinage of silver at the current ratio of 16 to 1. Next to the coinage question, the most important issue of the campaign was that of the duties on imports, McKinley representing those who desired higher tariffs as a protection to domestic industries.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the first important act during the McKinley administration?
answer: The calling of an extra session of Congress in March, 1897, for the enactment of a new protective tariff, styled the Dingley Act, in honor of Representative Dingley of Maine, its author. The act was signed July 24, 1898.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Did either House of Congress do anything else of note at the extra session?
answer: The Senate rejected a general treaty of arbitration between Great Britain and the United States.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Was this the only treaty of consequence before the Senate at this session?
answer: No. A treaty with the republic of Hawaii, for its annexation to the United States, was sent in by President McKinley, but went over till the regular session the following winter and then failed.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How did its failure affect Hawaiian annexation?
answer: It made it necessary to effect the annexation by joint resolution as in the case of Texas. This was finally accomplished in the summer of 1898.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What remarkable separation occurred between the prices of silver and wheat in 1897?
answer: Wheat rose in price to $1.19 cents a bushel, and silver fell to 45 1/16 cents an ounce.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Was any effort made to improve the value of silver?
answer: An international monetary commission was appointed by President McKinley. It visited England, France and Germany in behalf of an agreement between those countries and the United States for maintaining their silver coinage at a stated ratio of value as compared with gold; but their efforts were fruitless.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What noteworthy event for New York occurred in 1897?
answer: The enactment of the Greater New York law by the State Legislature, uniting Brooklyn, Staten Island and other adjacent territory with New York City proper, and raising it in population to the second rank, as to both area and population, among the cities of the world. The consolidation went into effect January 1, 1898.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What important general legislation was enacted by the Fifty-fifth Congress at its winter session of 1897-1898?
answer: A bankruptcy law; resolutions declaring Cuba free, and thus bringing about a war with Spain; and the War Revenue Act.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: How long did the war with Spain last?
answer: Actual hostilities began April 21, 1898, with the severance of diplomatic intercourse, and ended August 12, 1898, with the signing of a peace protocol at Washington.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the principal battles in this war?
answer: The battle of Manila Bay, on May 1, in which Admiral Dewey destroyed the entire Spanish fleet in Philippine waters; the sea-fight off the mouth of the harbor of Santiago, Cuba, July 3, when the ships under Admirals Schley and Sampson destroyed the fleet of the Spanish Admiral Cervera; the battles incidental to the capture of Santiago, between June 24 and July 17, and the battle which ended the siege of Manila and brought about its surrender, fought just before the news of the peace negotiations reached the Philippines.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What other exhibitions of American heroism distinguished this war?
answer: The sinking of the collier Merrimac, in the narrowest part of the entrance to the harbor of Santiago, to blockade the harbor. This was accomplished by Naval Constructor Hobson, with a picked crew of seven men, at night, under fire of the Spanish shore batteries. Col. Funston's bravery in the Philippines was also especially notable.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What brought about this war?
answer: The state of disquietude in the United States caused by the long and bloody war carried on by the Spanish army in Cuba in its attempt to put down the rebellion there. The acme of outraged sentiment in the United States was reached when, on February 15, the American battleship Maine was destroyed in the harbor of Havana by a mysterious explosion, causing the death of 264 American officers and seamen.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the principal terms of the peace protocol?
answer: The cession of Porto Rico by Spain to the United States; the evacuation of that island and of Cuba; the renunciation of Spanish sovereignty in Cuba; and the reference of the future disposal of the Philippine Islands to an international conference or Peace Commission.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who were appointed to represent the United States in the peace conference, and where did the conference hold its sessions?
answer: Senators Davis, Frye and Gray; William R. Day, late Secretary of State, and Whitelaw Reid of New York. The sessions of the conference were held in Paris.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the character of the War Revenue Act?
answer: It provided for the issue of bonds to raise money for carrying on the war, and for a far-reaching system of internal taxation to supply the means for meeting the principal and interest of the debt thus created and supply the government with additional revenue.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What did the treaty of peace provide?
answer: That Spain should give up all her remaining possessions in the Western Hemisphere, as forecast by the protocol, and cede to the United States the Philippine Archipelago in consideration of the payment of $20,000,000 by the United States, and also the island of Guam in the Ladrones; and that each government should relinquish all its own claims for indemnity and those of its citizens or subjects, growing out of the Cuban war. The treaty contained also some provisions securing especially favorable commercial privileges to Spain in her late possessions transferred to the United States.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: When was peace formally declared?
answer: On April 11, 1899, when the ratifications of the treaty by the two nations were exchanged at the Executive Mansion in Washington.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What dramatic coincidence attended the vote of the United States Senate for the ratification of the treaty?
answer: The struggle over ratification had been very fierce, a large number of Senators objecting to the assumption of sovereignty by the United States Government in the Philippines. On February 4, 1899, after the 6th had been fixed as the date for the final vote, the Filipino insurgent army, under General Emilio Aguinaldo, opened fire upon the American army in the outposts of Manila. This was obviously done for the purpose of emphasizing the demand of the Filipinos for independence as opposed to annexation to the United States. It seemed to have an effect the reverse of what was intended. Some of the Senators who had been inclined to listen to the plea of the Filipinos now declared that this violence must be punished. When the vote was taken on February 6, it stood 57 to 27, or one more than the requisite two-thirds in favor of the treaty.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Has the United States any other foreign complications on hand at present?
answer: Yes. A joint commission representing Great Britain, Canada and the United States, which met in Washington in the fall of 1898, suspended its sessions for a time in the following spring, because, in considering the various subjects which had caused irritation between the United States and the other countries involved, it could not reach a satisfactory agreement on a boundary line between Alaska and the British possessions in the American Northwest. In Samoa, also, trouble has grown out of the tripartite system of government, the British and American repre sentatives espousing the cause of Malietoa, one of the claimants to the Samoan throne, while the German representatives sided with his rival, Mataafa. The dissension became so violent that Rear-Admiral Kautz, of the United States Navy, was sent to Apia with a suitable force and was obliged to do some fighting with the natives of Mataafa's faction.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the cost to the United States of the Spanish-American war?
answer: The loss of 402 lives in battle and an expense of $165,000,000.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What was the Homestead Act?
answer: A bill passed by Congress in 1862, making a gift of 160 acres of public land to every bona fide settler who moves upon the same and improves and cultivates it. Many persons took advantage of this act during McKinley's administration.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What does The Hague peace conference treaty provide?
answer: It provides that the United States, Japan, and the principal nations of Europe shall maintain a perpetual peace court at the city of The Hague, where contested questions between these nations may be arbitrated.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What can you say about the Panama Canal?
answer: The United States paid to the republic of Panama the sum of $10,000,000 for the control of a strip of land across their country, ten miles in width, and then paid the French government $40,000,000 for what they had already performed on the canal.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What national exposition occurred in 1901?
answer: The Pan-American exposition at Buffalo, N. Y. As the word pan means all, so all the countries of both North and South America participated.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What public calamity occurred at this exposition?
answer: The assassination, on Sept. 6, 1901, of President McKin-ley by a youth of foreign parentage, Czolgosz by name.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What noted achievement was accomplished in 1902?
answer: The laying of a marine cable from San Francisco to Hawaii and thence to Manila. On July 4th of the fol-lowing year President Roosevelt sent a message around the world via San Francisco, Hawaii, Manila, Hongkong, and New York.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What noted achievement opened up the year 1903?
answer: The sending, from the station on Cape Cod, Massachu-setts, of a message by wireless telegraph from President Roosevelt to King Edward VII. of England.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What event in history did the St. Louis Exposition commemorate?
answer: The one hundredth anniversary of the purchase of the Louisiana territory from France by the United States in 1803.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What historical event did the Portland, Oregon, Ex-position commemorate?
answer: The exploring expedition of Lewis and Clark, which in 1805 gave us a title to Oregon by right of discovery and exploration.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Give in brief the happenings of this administration.
answer: 1. Congress enacting the Dingley High Tariff Bill.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What four calamities occurred during this adminis-tration?
answer: 1. Galveston hurricane and tidal wave in 1900, that cost the lives of more than 6,000 people, and destroyed property valued at $18,000,000. 2. In 1904 the Baltimore conflagration with a property loss of $50,000,000. 3. The San Francisco earthquake in 1906, in which 200,000 people were made homeless, and $400,000,000 of property, destroyed. 4. In 1907 the country suffered a short but severe money panic, which happily only lasted a few months.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What were the results of the next presidential elec-tion?
answer: The Republican party was victorious at the general elec-tion in November, 1908, and elected William H. Taft, of Ohio, President, and James S. Sherman, of New York, Vice-President, over William J. Bryan, of Nebraska, and John W. Kern, of Indiana, the candidates of the Democratic party.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: Who compose Taft's cabinet?
answer: Sec. of state, Philander C. Knox, of Pa. Sec. of the treas., Franklin MacVeagh, of Ill. Sec. of war, Jacob M. Dickinson, of Tenn. Atty.-gen., G. W. Wickersham, of New York. Postmaster-gen., Frank Hitchcock, of Mass. Sec. of the navy, Geo. Von L. Meyer, of Mass. Sec. of the int., R. A. Ballinger, of Wash. Sec. of agri., James Wilson, of Iowa. Sec. of commerce and labor, Chas. Nagel, of Mo. Salary of sec. of state, $8000; of the other cabinet members, $12,000 each.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What are some of the important acts of the first part of this administration?
answer: 1. The passing of an enabling act by Congress allowing the Territories of New Mexico and Arizona to come into the Union as States upon their formulating and adopting constitutions satisfactory to Congress and in no respect opposed to the Constitution of the United States. 2. The enacting into law of the bill known as the Payne Tariff Bill. 3. Passing a bill permitting postal savings banks to be established. 4. Conservation of natural resources. 5. Publication of campaign expenses by candidates for office.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What exploring achievements were reported in the year 1909?
answer: 1. Dr. Frederick A. Cook, returning from a cruise in the Arctic regions, claimed that on April 21, 1908, he reached the north pole and that no land was found beyond the 86th parallel and the thermometer registered 83 degrees below zero (Fahr.). Cook failed to substantiate his claims. 2. Commodore Peary of the U. S. Navy also claimed that on April 6, 1909, he discovered the north pole, and that no land was visible after the 86th parallel, and the thermometer registered 83 degrees below zero. Peary's claims have received general credence by the people of all countries, but Cook's claims have been generally discredited. 3. About the same time Lieut. Shackleton of England approached to within one hundred miles of the south pole. Two years after this the pole was actually discovered by Capt. Roald Amundsen who spent from Dec. 14-17, 1911, taking observations on the spot.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What about rural free delivery?
answer: It was introduced first in 1895 in the United States, and now fully 18,000,000 people living in country districts, by virtue of its beneficent provisions receive their mail daily at their homes.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What important strides have been made in the past ten years in regard to aviation?
answer: On Dec. 17, 1903, Wilbur and Orville Wright, of Dayton, O., made the first flight in a motor-driven aëro-plane and traveled 852 ft. and remained in the air 59 seconds. Shortly after this, on Nov. 13, 1906, one Santos-Dumont made a short flight near Paris, France. On July 25, 1909, an aviator by the name of Bleriot flew across the English Channel, and shortly after this another aviator traveled 144 miles before alighting. The statistics in 1910 show that there are fully 500 styles of aëroplanes now being manufactured, and in France alone there are now 17 factories building and equipping machines, while there are 19 factories in England, 4 in Germany and 3 in the United States, and it is estimated that fully $20,000,-000 and 20,000 people are engaged in their manufacture and development.
section: QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON UNITED STATES HISTORY

question: What is the longest trip without alighting that has been made by the aëroplane?
answer: One hundred and forty-four miles.
